faq.a: The reluctor ring (or tone wheel) is a toothed wheel attached to the crankshaft. As its teeth pass the sensor, they generate electrical pulses that the ECM uses to determine crank position and speed.

faq.q: Can a damaged flexplate cause P0336?
faq.a: Yes. On some vehicles, the CKP sensor reads teeth on the flywheel or flexplate. A cracked or damaged flexplate can produce erratic sensor signals.

faq.q: Why does my car stall randomly with this code?
faq.a: An erratic CKP signal causes the ECM to momentarily lose track of engine position, disrupting fuel injection and ignition timing, which can cause the engine to stall.

title: Camshaft Position Sensor A
shortDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the camshaft position sensor A circuit (Bank 1).
fullDescription: Code P0340 indicates the ECM is not receiving a proper signal from the camshaft position sensor (CMP) on Bank 1. The CMP sensor works with the CKP sensor to determine the exact position of the engine in its four-stroke cycle, enabling sequential fuel injection. Loss of this signal may cause the ECM to default to batch-fire injection, reducing efficiency.
safetyImpact: Engine may run rough, have difficulty starting, or stall. Performance is reduced.

causes.cause: Faulty camshaft reluctor ring or target
Timing chain stretch affecting cam-to-crank correlation

symptoms: Hard starting or extended crank time

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the CMP sensor connector for corrosion, damage, or loose fit.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the CMP sensor resistance and compare to specifications. Check for voltage output while cranking.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Verify power supply, ground, and signal wire integrity between the sensor and ECM.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the camshaft position sensor. Ensure proper installation and alignment.

faq.q: Will the engine still run without a cam sensor?
faq.a: Many engines can still start and run without a cam sensor signal by defaulting to batch-fire injection (injecting all cylinders simultaneously). Performance and efficiency will be reduced.

faq.q: Where is the camshaft position sensor?
faq.a: The CMP sensor is typically located near the camshaft, often at the front of the cylinder head, near the timing cover, or in the distributor housing on older engines.

faq.q: Can a stretched timing chain cause this code?
faq.a: Yes. A stretched timing chain changes the relationship between crankshaft and camshaft positions, which can cause the CMP signal to fall outside expected parameters.

title: CMP Sensor A Range/Performance
shortDescription: The camshaft position sensor A signal is outside the expected range or does not correlate properly with the crankshaft position sensor.
fullDescription: Code P0341 is set when the ECM detects that the camshaft position sensor A signal does not match expected patterns relative to the crankshaft position sensor. The signal may be present but has incorrect timing, erratic patterns, or does not properly correlate with engine position. This often indicates a mechanical timing issue.
safetyImpact: Incorrect cam-to-crank correlation can cause poor engine performance, hard starting, and potential valve timing issues.

causes.cause: Stretched timing chain or worn timing belt
Camshaft phaser or VVT actuator malfunction

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the CMP sensor if resistance is out of spec. This is the easiest potential fix.

fixSteps.title: Check Timing Correlation
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to compare CMP and CKP sensor signals. Large deviations indicate a mechanical timing issue.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Timing Components
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the timing chain/belt for stretch, slack, or wear. Check timing marks for proper alignment.

fixSteps.title: Repair Timing
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the timing chain/belt and tensioner if stretched or worn. Replace VVT components if faulty.

faq.q: Is this code always a sensor problem?
faq.a: No. While the sensor can be faulty, P0341 often points to a mechanical issue like a stretched timing chain that changes the relationship between the camshaft and crankshaft.

faq.q: How do I know if my timing chain is stretched?
faq.a: A rattling noise at startup, poor performance, and P0341 combined with P0016 (cam/crank correlation) are strong indicators of timing chain stretch.

faq.q: Is timing chain replacement expensive?
faq.a: Yes. It is labor-intensive, often requiring 4-8 hours. Total cost typically ranges from $800-2,000 depending on the vehicle.

title: Camshaft Position Sensor A Bank 2
shortDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the camshaft position sensor A circuit on Bank 2.
fullDescription: Code P0345 indicates a malfunction in the camshaft position sensor circuit on Bank 2 of the engine. This applies to V-type engines that have separate camshaft position sensors for each bank. The ECM is not receiving a valid signal from the Bank 2 CMP sensor, which affects sequential fuel injection on that bank.
safetyImpact: Reduced engine performance on Bank 2. Engine may have difficulty starting or stall.

fixSteps.title: Check Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the Bank 2 CMP sensor connector for corrosion and proper fit.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure sensor resistance and check for signal output during cranking.

fixSteps.title: Verify Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check wiring integrity between the sensor and ECM.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the Bank 2 camshaft position sensor.

faq.q: Which side is Bank 2?
faq.a: Bank 2 is the side of the engine that does not contain cylinder 1. On most V-type engines, consult the service manual for the specific bank assignment.

faq.q: Should I replace both cam sensors if one fails?
faq.a: It is not required but can be cost-effective if one bank's sensor is already being worked on and the sensors are the same age and mileage.

faq.q: Can this code be caused by low oil?
faq.a: On engines with VVT (variable valve timing), low oil pressure can affect the camshaft phaser operation and potentially cause CMP sensor related codes.

title: Ignition Coil Primary/Secondary
shortDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the ignition coil primary or secondary circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0350 is a general ignition coil circuit malfunction code that does not specify a particular cylinder. It indicates the ECM has detected a fault in an ignition coil's primary or secondary circuit. This may be set on vehicles where the ECM monitors the overall ignition system rather than individual coils.
safetyImpact: Ignition system faults cause misfires that can damage the catalytic converter and reduce power.

causes.cause: Damaged ignition coil connector or wiring
Faulty ignition module or ECM driver
Bad ground connection to ignition coil

fixSteps.title: Identify Faulty Coil
fixSteps.instruction: If not specified by a companion code, use a scan tool to identify which cylinder is misfiring to narrow down the coil.

fixSteps.title: Test Coil Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the primary and secondary resistance of each ignition coil. Compare to specifications.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect power supply and trigger signal wiring to the ignition coils.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty ignition coil and clear codes.

faq.q: Is P0350 the same as P0351?
faq.a: P0350 is a general ignition coil circuit code, while P0351 specifies coil A (cylinder 1). P0350 is used on some vehicles that do not have cylinder-specific coil monitoring.

faq.q: How do I test an ignition coil?
faq.a: Measure primary resistance (typically 0.5-2 ohms) and secondary resistance (typically 5,000-15,000 ohms) with a multimeter. Values outside these ranges indicate a faulty coil.

faq.q: Can a bad spark plug damage an ignition coil?
faq.a: Yes. A worn or fouled spark plug with excessive gap forces the coil to produce higher voltage, which accelerates coil wear and can cause premature failure.

title: Ignition Coil A Circuit
shortDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the ignition coil A (cylinder 1) primary or secondary circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0351 indicates the ECM has detected a fault in the ignition coil circuit for cylinder 1 (coil A). This can be a primary side fault (ECM-to-coil circuit) or a secondary side fault (coil-to-spark plug circuit). The ECM monitors the coil circuit for proper operation and sets this code when the circuit does not behave as expected.
safetyImpact: Cylinder 1 will misfire, causing power loss and potential catalytic converter damage.

causes.cause: Worn or fouled spark plug causing coil overload

fixSteps.title: Swap Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Swap coil A with another coil. If the code moves to the new cylinder, the coil is faulty.

fixSteps.title: Check Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect and replace the cylinder 1 spark plug. A bad plug can cause coil failure.

fixSteps.title: Test Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the coil connector for power, ground, and trigger signal.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty ignition coil A.

faq.q: How long do ignition coils last?
faq.a: Ignition coils typically last 80,000-120,000 miles but can fail earlier due to heat, vibration, or spark plug issues.

faq.q: Should I always replace the spark plug with the coil?
faq.a: It is best practice. A worn spark plug increases the voltage demand on the coil and can cause the new coil to fail prematurely.

faq.q: Can I use an aftermarket coil?
faq.a: Quality aftermarket coils work fine. Avoid very cheap coils as they may not meet OEM voltage and durability specifications.

title: Ignition Coil B Circuit
shortDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the ignition coil B (cylinder 2) primary or secondary circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0352 indicates the ECM has detected a fault in the ignition coil circuit for cylinder 2 (coil B). The coil may be open or shorted, the wiring may be damaged, or the ECM driver circuit may have a fault. Cylinder 2 will not receive proper spark.
safetyImpact: Cylinder 2 misfire causes power loss and catalytic converter damage risk.

fixSteps.title: Swap Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Swap coil B with another cylinder's coil to confirm the fault.

fixSteps.title: Replace Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the cylinder 2 spark plug as a worn plug can cause coil failure.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the coil B connector and wiring for damage or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new ignition coil B.

faq.q: Is replacing an ignition coil a DIY job?
faq.a: Yes. On most vehicles with coil-on-plug ignition, replacing a coil involves removing a bolt and unplugging a connector. It is one of the simplest engine repairs.

faq.q: Can moisture cause coil failure?
faq.a: Yes. Water intrusion into the coil boot or connector can cause arcing and coil failure. Inspect for moisture if the vehicle has been driven in heavy rain or washed in the engine bay.

faq.q: Should I replace all coils if one fails?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Unlike spark plugs, coils do not wear uniformly. Replace only the failed coil, but keep a spare in the vehicle if desired.

title: Ignition Coil C Circuit
shortDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the ignition coil C (cylinder 3) primary or secondary circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0353 indicates the ECM has found a fault in the ignition coil C circuit for cylinder 3. The coil's primary or secondary winding may be open or shorted, the wiring may have a fault, or the connector may be damaged. This results in no spark on cylinder 3.
safetyImpact: Cylinder 3 misfire reduces power and can damage the catalytic converter.

fixSteps.title: Swap Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Swap coil C with a known good coil from another cylinder.

fixSteps.title: Check Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect and replace the cylinder 3 spark plug.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the coil C connector and wiring for damage, corrosion, or loose fit.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Replace ignition coil C if confirmed faulty.

faq.q: Can I test a coil without an oscilloscope?
faq.a: Yes. A basic test involves measuring primary resistance (0.5-2 ohms) and secondary resistance (5,000-15,000 ohms) with a multimeter. Swapping coils is the quickest diagnostic method.

faq.q: What if the code does not follow the coil when swapped?
faq.a: If the misfire stays on cylinder 3 after swapping the coil, the problem is likely the spark plug, injector, wiring, or a mechanical issue rather than the coil.

faq.q: Does a failing coil always set a code?
faq.a: Not always. A coil that is breaking down under load may cause intermittent misfires without immediately setting a code. The misfire has to exceed the ECM's threshold to trigger the code.

title: Ignition Coil D Circuit
shortDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the ignition coil D (cylinder 4) primary or secondary circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0354 indicates the ECM has detected a fault in the ignition coil D circuit for cylinder 4. This is a circuit-level code meaning the ECM detected an abnormality in the electrical circuit controlling coil D. The coil may be failed, or the wiring/connector may have a fault.
safetyImpact: Cylinder 4 misfire reduces power and can overheat the catalytic converter.

causes.cause: Bad spark plug causing coil stress
Wiring fault in coil D circuit

fixSteps.title: Swap Coil Test
fixSteps.instruction: Swap ignition coil D with another cylinder. Clear codes and see if the code follows the coil.

fixSteps.title: Replace Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the cylinder 4 spark plug with a properly gapped new plug.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the coil D connector and wiring for damage. Verify power supply and trigger signal.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new ignition coil D if confirmed faulty through the swap test.

faq.q: What is the coil swap test?
faq.a: Move the suspect coil to a different cylinder. If the misfire code changes to the new cylinder, the coil is bad. If the misfire stays on cylinder 4, the coil is not the problem.

faq.q: Can heat damage ignition coils?
faq.a: Yes. Ignition coils mounted directly on spark plugs (COP design) are exposed to significant engine heat, which degrades the insulation and windings over time.

faq.q: Are ignition coil D and cylinder 4 always the same?
faq.a: On most modern vehicles with coil-on-plug ignition, each coil letter corresponds to its cylinder number. However, on some waste-spark systems, one coil may fire two cylinders.

title: Ignition Coil E Circuit
shortDescription: The ECM has detected a malfunction in the ignition coil E (cylinder 5) primary or secondary circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0355 indicates the ECM has detected a fault in the ignition coil E circuit for cylinder 5. This code applies to engines with 5 or more cylinders. The coil may be internally failed, the wiring may be damaged, or the connector may be faulty, resulting in no spark on cylinder 5.
safetyImpact: Cylinder 5 misfire reduces power and risks catalytic converter damage from unburned fuel.

fixSteps.title: Swap Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Swap ignition coil E with a known good coil from another cylinder to confirm the fault.

fixSteps.title: Replace Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the cylinder 5 spark plug.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect coil E wiring and connector for damage or corrosion. Verify power supply and signal.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Replace ignition coil E if confirmed faulty.

faq.q: Can a coil fail intermittently?
faq.a: Yes. Ignition coils can fail when hot and work again when cool. This causes intermittent misfires that may be hard to reproduce during diagnosis.

faq.q: Does cylinder 5 always correspond to coil E?
faq.a: On coil-on-plug systems, yes. On waste-spark or distributor-based systems, the relationship may differ. Consult the service manual.

faq.q: How can I prevent coil failures?
faq.a: Replace spark plugs on schedule, use quality fuel, and keep the ignition system clean and dry. Avoid pressure-washing the engine bay near coil packs.
